steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Grasby et al.’s research question is whether perceived category similarity predicts consumers’ likelihood of purchasing brand extensions. The team already calculated probability changes for 30 extended-brand pairs. The missing piece is a direct measure of how similar consumers perceive the two product categories to be.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair.: This supplies the missing variable — a direct consumer rating of category similarity — and connects it to the probability data already in hand. That directly tests the research question. ✓
  • Choice B — Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition of each brand in the extended-brand pairs, then determine how, if at all, the degree of brand recognition correlates with the average cost of each product in the pairs.: Brand recognition is not category similarity, and average cost is unrelated to purchase probability. Neither variable addresses the research question. ✗
  • Choice C — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of one product in each extended-brand pair to other products in the same category, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair.: This measures within-category similarity, not between-category similarity (e.g., shoes vs. gloves). It does not test whether different categories feel related to consumers. ✗
  • Choice D — Poll a representative sample of the households to determine the degree of brand recognition for each brand in the extended-brand pairs, then determine how, if at all, the degree of brand recognition correlates with the frequency with which a different group of households purchased at least one product of that brand.: Brand recognition still isn’t category similarity, and using a different group’s purchase data introduces a mismatch. ✗
That matches Choice A — Have a representative sample of the households rate the similarity of the product categories in each extended-brand pair, then determine how, if at all, those ratings correlate with the change in probability that the team calculated for each pair..
